title: Design YouTube
overview: Build YouTube's video platform — upload, store, transcode, and serve video at scale to billions of users worldwide.

levels.title: Basic Video Platform
levels.constraints: 1K video uploads per day. Users upload a video and others can watch it. No transcoding — serve the original file.
levels.feedbackHint: Good start! Serving raw video files from a single server won't scale, and different devices need different resolutions.
levels.aiContextHint: Level 1 is a basic upload-and-serve design. The server stores video metadata in a DB and serves the raw video file. Accept any server + database design.

levels.title: Global Video Delivery
levels.constraints: 10M daily active viewers worldwide. Video playback buffers constantly for users far from the server. Bandwidth costs are skyrocketing.
levels.feedbackHint: CDN edge nodes cache and serve video segments globally — this is how YouTube delivers low-latency playback. Next: what about video transcoding?
levels.aiContextHint: Level 2 adds a CDN to cache and serve video segments from edge nodes near the viewer. The API Gateway handles routing. The origin server stores the master copy.

levels.title: Async Video Transcoding
levels.constraints: Uploaded videos must be converted to multiple resolutions (360p, 720p, 1080p, 4K) and formats. Transcoding blocks the upload response for minutes — unacceptable.
levels.feedbackHint: Async transcoding via a job queue decouples upload from processing. Workers pick up jobs and produce multiple renditions. The video is 'processing' until complete.
levels.aiContextHint: Level 3 uses an async transcoding pipeline. After upload, the server enqueues a transcoding job (NoSQL queue). Worker services consume jobs, produce multiple video formats, and write results back. Metadata (status, rendition URLs) is stored in the DB.

levels.title: Scale to Billions
levels.constraints: 500 hours of video uploaded per minute. Metadata queries (search, recommendations, comments) are slow. A single relational DB can't handle the mixed workload.
levels.feedbackHint: You've designed a YouTube-scale system! Separating relational (structured metadata) from NoSQL (comments, view counts) and adding a cache for hot data is the key to handling this mixed workload.
levels.aiContextHint: Level 4 uses polyglot persistence: RDBMS for structured metadata (videos, channels), NoSQL for high-write data (view counts, comments, watch history), and a cache for hot queries. CDN continues to serve video segments.
